title: Executive Order 12072—Federal Space Management

flags.justification: This revokes EO 11512 (Sec. 1-303) and replaces its formal thirty-day appeal-to-the-President mechanism with a weaker 'notify and consider additional information' step (Sec. 1-203(e)) — a real increase in the Administrator's effectively unchecked discretion relative to the predecessor order.
The loss of the formal appeal mechanism from EO 11512, with no comparable substitute, is a genuine accountability gap relative to its predecessor.
Multiple related urban-development and space-management criteria all coherently serve one purpose.

The extensive multi-factor urban-impact criteria (Sec. 1-104: local development compatibility, economic impact, housing availability, transportation access) require real judgment with no defined weighting process.
No incentive concern distinct from the vagueness already captured under Flag 4.
A standing administrative function, evergreen by nature; supersedes a prior order (EO 11512), evidencing periodic revision.

This revokes EO 11512's formal appeal-to-the-President mechanism and replaces it with a weaker notify-and-consider process — a genuine, identifiable removal of an existing procedural safeguard, distinct from the more common pattern in this corpus of successor orders adding rather than removing checks.

Sec. 1-203(c) requires consultation with Federal, State, regional, and local officials and consideration of their objections — real, if less formal than its predecessor's appeal right, external input.
This embeds space acquisition within established Carter-era urban policy goals; it implements rather than exceeds existing policy direction.
Sec. 1-203(b) requires coordination with OMB; the order is generally well-coordinated with other agencies' input.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 4/20 = 20.0%. Notable as a rare example in this corpus of a successor order regressing on a specific procedural safeguard — EO 11512's formal thirty-day presidential appeal right does not carry over here, replaced by a weaker consultation requirement, contrary to the more common pattern of successor orders adding rather than removing checks.
